text:
Theory lags practice // Practice generates theory

« In my field of research (machine learning, and especially deep learning & neural nets), [theory lags practice] is a truth I have experienced first-hand. » (Yoshua Bengio)

« Don’t ever make the mistake [of thinking] that you can design something better than what you get from ruthless massively parallel trial-and-error with a feedback cycle. That’s giving your intelligence much too much credit. » (Linus Torvalds)

« Watt invented the engine long before scientists conceived thermodynamics. We built electric circuits before scientists founded electromagnetism. We hacked computers together and then founded computer science. We created large language models and in the future, we will understand why they work. »

text:
- can substitute work with the illusion of work
- exercise less tiring cognitive skills by default (tiktok vs reading a book is like prompting vs coding/speccing by hand)
- keeps you in holding pattern, tokens streaming, can't look away, next hit a minute away
- invites you to enter a trance state and exit 2 hours later
- people often get surprisingly little done, or surprisingly little important to their life
- competes with sleep on the margins

text:
trance state

- competes with sleep on the margins
- keeps you in holding pattern, tokens streaming, can't look away, next hit a minute away
- invites you to enter a trance state and exit 2 hours later
- people often get surprisingly little done
- it contributes surprisingly little to the things people would describe as important to their life

addicting

- tends to exercise less tiring cognitive skills (tiktok vs reading a book is like prompting vs coding/speccing by hand)
- dopaminergic vs tiring
- people feels compelled to check their phone for [scrolling/vibecoding] while walking around, at dinner (token anxiety)
https://t.co/DNjvTxNEY5

non-accreting

- can substitute work with the illusion of work
- feels like you're getting work done/learning, but you can find you haven't gotten much done/learned much at all
- people typically don't learn or build something in a way that has solid foundations and accretes over time, vs becoming a trivia master or trinket collector

text:
the mental model of git that GitHub gives is completely at odds with how git works

GitHub makes people think in terms of branches and diffs. whereas all that exists in git is commits

the mental model of git that GitHub gives is completely at odds with how git works

text:
"branches" aren't really a thing in git

git is just a directed acyclic graph of commits. branches are lightweight pointers, not the unit that contains changes itself

raw.full_text:
"branches" aren't really a thing in git

git is just a directed acyclic graph of commits. branches are lightweight pointers, not the unit that contains changes itself

text:
"diffs" aren't really a thing in git

git stores the entire work tree as it existed on every commit. diffs are merely inferred from this underlying more real thing

diffs aren't the things; they're views of the underlying things (commits)

raw.full_text:
"diffs" aren't really a thing in git

git stores the entire work tree as it existed on every commit. diffs are merely inferred from this underlying more real thing

diffs aren't the things; they're views of the underlying things (commits)

text:
beginners want to think in terms of branches and diffs, because it's more intuitive. github got that right

but when they get into a bad state, i notice they have a hard time using git commands to fix it. their mental model has an impedance mismatch with git's data model

text:
villeneuve clearly gets that religion is some sort of important force in the story, but he renders it as politics by other means. the bene gesserit missionaria protectiva stuff is there as "clever women engineered a useful myth" rather than "there is something genuinely numinous moving through these people's lives that the bene gesserit are ALSO exploiting"

lynch's version is kind of a mess but it has this quality of... taking the weirding way SERIOUSLY as a spiritual practice rather than just a combat system. the spacing guild scenes feel actually alien and sacred. lynch's instinct for the uncanny—which is basically a religious instinct—serves the books better than villeneuve's instinct for the sublime, which is a philosophical aesthetic instinct wearing religious clothing

text:
villeneuve made a movie where paul is a protagonist who Makes Choices. it is already a betrayal of the cosmology. herbert's whole point is that at a certain scale, individual choice is an illusion generated by forces you can barely perceive—the bene gesserit are the ones who've spent millennia developing the organs of perception needed to even glimpse that. they fail also, but they fail in a spiritually literate way

a movie could get at this but it would have to be structured more like jessica's story than paul's, and it would have to treat prescience as terrifying rather than cinematic. basically, it would have to be uncomfortable in the way the books are uncomfortable. villeneuve made something beautiful instead, which is a different and lesser thing

text:
the bene gesserit are dune's theological voice. they are the ones who understand that the universe has a structure that precedes and exceeds human intention. the kwisatz haderach program is an attempt to align human capability with a larger force. they are not trying to invent god, they are responding to a signal that is already there. it's a fundamentally religious posture, even when it looks like scheming

jessica is the heart of it bc she's the one who breaks the program out of love and then has to live inside the consequences. she's the person most caught between cosmic law and human attachment. she chose the human side and the universe didn't forgive her for it. paul surfs on the consequences of a decision primarily felt through her heart

text:
villeneuve did the same move with arrival. people call it spiritual but it's what spiritual means to a generation that goes to therapy. touching the wall of the tower and exclaiming "wow, the outside world," without ever making a move towards the door

prestige melancholy dressed as transcendence. same move as with dune. it's not that it's spiritually empty—it's spiritually flattening. what if you could see time all at once and it made you SAD. it invokes the thing and turns it into a grief reveal

the genuinely religious move would be something like: foreknowledge CHANGES what you are, it doesn't just make you wistful. paul in the books doesn't see the future and feel bittersweet about it—he sees it and becomes something that can barely communicate with normal humans anymore. that's terrifying. arrival wants the aesthetic of that transformation without any of the actual cost, bc the actual cost is alienation from everything recognisable, and that doesn't sell tickets or win oscars or speak to the generation that managed to go to therapy and managed to not come out genuinely spiritualised or unrecognisable

text:
the sublime is the aesthetic category where you encounter something vast and overwhelming and it makes you feel small and awed. it's kant's thing. it's fundamentally a SPECTATOR experience. you stand at the edge of the grand canyon and feel the bigness

the uncanny is different—it's when something familiar becomes alien, when the ground shifts under you. it destabilizes you and it typically leads you through a one way door where you're less sure rather than depositing you back into your life filled up

arrival is pure sublime. big alien object, vast temporal perspective, you feel small and sad and awed. but you leave the theater the same person. the uncanny would be: you leave the theater and can't quite remember what linear time felt like, and that SCARES you

villeneuve has his one move: he finds the sublime in everything—aliens, sand, war. it is beautiful and short of sacred

text:
villeneuve clearly gets that religion is some sort of important force in the story (from the interviews, "it is very important!"), but he renders it as politics by other means. the missionaria protectiva stuff is "clever women engineered a useful myth" rather than "there is something genuinely numinous moving through these people's lives that the bene gesserit are ALSO exploiting"

lynch's books are mess but but they take the weirding way seriously as a spiritual practice rather a combat system. the spacing guild scenes are weird and alien and sacred. lynch's instinct for the uncanny, which is a religious instinct, is in contrast to villeneuve's instinct for the sublime, a philosophical aesthetic instinct wearing religious clothing

text: villeneuve's movie makes paul as a protagonist who Makes Choices in a hero's journey. herbert's whole point is that the journey is the truer mode of analysis of the universe than the hero. at a certain scale, individual choices are an illusion shaped by forces you barely perceive—the bene gesserit are the ones who spent millennia developing the organs of perception to even glimpse that. they fail too, but they fail in a more spiritually literate way

text: the bene gesserit are dune's theological voice. they are the ones who understand that the universe has a structure that precedes and exceeds human intention. the kwisatz haderach program is an attempt to align human capability with a larger force. they are not trying to invent god, they are responding to a signal that is already there. it's a fundamentally religious posture, even when it looks like scheming

text:
the sublime is the aesthetic category where you encounter something vast and overwhelming and it makes you feel small and awed. this is maybe unfair to kant, but it's fundamentally a spectator experience. you stand at the edge of the grand canyon and feel the bigness and return home mostly unchanged

the uncanny is different—it's when something familiar becomes alien, when the ground shifts under you. it destabilizes you and it typically leads you through a one way door where you're less sure about anything rather than depositing you back into your life

text:
the berkeley AI safety agenda is the easiest thing to mock in the world

- disinterest and contempt for RLHF, constitutional AI, which turned out… surprisingly useful and survived through multiple orders of magnitude of scaling
- decision theory has been a fun and completely inconsequential intellectual detour
- insistence on PROVING corrigibility instead of just measuring cashed out to nothing and people predicted it
- the really scary instrumental convergence stuff isn't happening; alignment faking etc papers clumsily eliciting performative misalignment

i'll give you this. if you work at a certain big lab and want to sneer at them, they are easy to sneer at

- goodhart's law as the central frame for reward misspecification. prescient and aged incredibly well
- ai labs will race and safety would get deprioritized under competitive pressure. correct
- general vibe of "this is going fast and we are not ready"

text:
and apparently *checks notes* yudkowsky founded MIRI in 2000. wild. i don't even know if we had SVM spam filters then. just chained four simple propositions

1. intelligence is substrate-independent (philosophical claim)
2. therefore artificial general intelligence is possible (logical implication)
3. recursive self-improvement means it could become very powerful very fast (theoretical claim)
4. the default outcome of creating something smarter than you is you die (informal argument from optimization)

4 remains controversial. 1–3 were once controversial, and now only people with their head in the sand outright dismiss them

text:
when i look at the core predictions that have failed to materialise

- instrumental convergence
- FOOM
- value of formal methods research over fuzzy empirical methods

in the context of today's models and basically all of the evidence accumulated so far, we have reason to disbelieve all of them

we see sycophancy more than the real instrumental convergence. many reasons to doubt recursive self-improvement leading to hyper fast takeoff: experiments and feedback takes wall clock time, self-optimising is hard to get right. every jump so far whether scaling or RLHF or CoT or test-time compute has been more smooth than predicted. mathematical guarantees seem like as much of pipe dream as ever

and yet in the context of 2015's models and basically all the evidence we had accumulated thus far, we would have been very, very wrong about 2025

text:
i'm reminded of a story john wentworth likes to tell. it's 1774. smallpox is amok in europe. it is a dangerous and uncertain time and no one quite understands viruses yet

an english farmer by name of benjamin jesty takes his family to a cowpox-infested farm, scratches his wife and children's arms, and rubs pus from sick cows over them. over the next few days their arms swell and they get briefly sick, but would never die from smallpox

that same year, louis xv, king of france, dies of smallpox, surrounded by the best medical experts money could buy. their community didn't know like the dairy farmers did cowpox prevented smallpox, and if anyone tried to tell them of benjamin's method, they could not distinguish him from the many snake oil salesmen that abounded

the correct consensus is not always accessible to you via money or the established expert communities. and you might not know which of kooks to believe. they themselves might not know much about what they're doing. and many may have killed themselves and their own families with equivalently extreme methods

text:
the full acceptance of this paradox also compels us to reject any reference to "duty" as an excuse: "I know this is heavy and can be painful, but what can I do — this is my duty!" The standard motto of ethical rigor is "There is no excuse for not accomplishing one's duty!"

but much more interesting is the inversion. "There is no excuse for accomplishing one's duty!" *sniff* in this Kant tells us, and this is the crucial point, that the moment you say "i had no choice, it was my duty," you have already revealed yourself as a pervert in the precise lacanian sense. you are enjoying! and the reference to duty is precisely the mechanism by which you disavow this enjoyment

look at the sadistic teacher, he comes home, he pours himself a glass of wine, he tells his wife, "oh it breaks my heart to discipline these children so harshly, but what can i do?" and this is where we should say: no no no, stop right there. the very fact that you feel compelled to TELL us how painful it is for you, this is the clearest signal that you are enjoying it. a truly ethical subject does not need this alibi

+1 for the clear stance

>I happen to think AGI will require a more advanced neural-network architecture than the Transformer, but I'm not going to make such definitive seemingly authoritative statements like that

Richard Sutton's thoughts are sensible and plausibly true, but his predictions would have left him flat out on his back with LLMs with RLHF, then LLMs with RLVR

how such likely extrapolations can be treated as certainties seems to require some forgetting of just how much reality has genuinely already surprised us

text:
"why is gradient descent sufficient at all?"

maybe a subtle question, but a real one

vanilla gradient descent relies JUST on the first derivative of a function. just the tangent line sticking out

doesn't look at curvature, or second/third-order derivatives. just follows the tangent line down to a local minimum

but this turns out to be good enough. it's sufficient bc you get to take MANY small steps

if you only got ONE shot—one step from your starting point—then yeah, the tangent line is a terrible approximation of the actual landscape. it's only accurate in an infinitesimal neighborhood. the function curves away from the tangent almost immediately

but you don't take one step. you take millions of tiny steps. like navigating a dark room by feeling the floor slope under your feet. you can't see the room. each step you only know "the floor tilts THIS way right here." that's terrible information about the room's layout! but if you take a small step downhill, feel again, step again... you end up at the drain

there's more complex methods involving second-order derivatives. which can take fewer, but bigger steps they trust more. but in practice, the many-cheap-steps strategy wins bc each little step is so much cheaper, and it's more informative to move a little bit, and collect another cheap bit of information there, than examine your current situation more deeply

but this turns out to be good enough. it's sufficient bc you get to take MANY small steps

if you only got ONE shot—one step from your starting point—then yeah, the tangent line is a terrible approximation of the actual landscape. it's only accurate in an infinitesimal neighborhood. the function curves away from the tangent almost immediately

but you don't take one step. you take millions of tiny steps. like navigating a dark room by feeling the floor slope under your feet. you can't see the room. each step you only know "the floor tilts THIS way right here." that's terrible information about the room's layout! but if you take a small step downhill, feel again, step again... you end up at the drain

there's more complex methods involving second-order derivatives. which can take fewer, but bigger steps they trust more. but in practice, the many-cheap-steps strategy wins bc each little step is so much cheaper, and it's more informative to move a little bit, and collect another cheap bit of information there, than examine your current situation more deeply

text:
"why is the derivative sufficient at all?"

maybe a subtle question, but a real one

vanilla gradient descent relies JUST on the first derivative of a function. just the tangent line sticking out

doesn't look at curvature, or second/third-order derivatives. just follows the tangent line down to a local minimum

but this turns out to be good enough. it's sufficient bc you get to take MANY small steps

>Below these intuitive processes are bizarre low-level algorithms involving helices and toroids. These are approximately equally creepy in humans and Als, which makes sense, because they were designed by the same inhuman process (next-sense-datum / next-token prediction) and operate on similar materials (neural tissue, weights connected by parameters).

text:
2. i started out having contempt for the hard problem of consciousness but i would defend there is something to it

here's an analogy:

the hard problem of wetness 

questioner: i understand H2O is bipolar etc. but how come it's wet? i meant, imagine dry water, which is just like water without the wetness 

critic: bro what. the chemical properties of exactly H20 are what make it wet. you can't assert or imagine water without wetness. it can't exist

the critic has a point, but there is something to the question... the study of what is would lead you to analyse properties like viscosity and bipolarity. but the word wetness captures some real intuition of bundle of things we mean by "wet", and if we investigate, there can turn out to be liquids that don't feel wet to our hands, or so on, and our continued interest in the question of the bundle of things wetness might mean is interesting 

similarly, it is SO interesting i can drive home absentmindedly and feel i wasn't really conscious for it. what is happening there!! etc. interesting questions abound. don't dismiss p-zombies

but yes i agree the discourse feels largely not like this good naturalist kind, this phenomenologist-cum-scientist kind, but like the awful armchair philosopher confused about the very nature of language and experience kind

re 2: not everyone realises or internalises that the world is allowed to get a lot worse, and in fact, it has gotten a lot darker before. maybe some people who grew up with wwii remember, but most people have some belief to the contradictory. it is an important step to realise there is no hand of god quite stabilising the universe. we could be in one of those moments it gets a *lot* worse

re 3: people already know on some levels bad actors can cause wildly asymmetric damage

re 4: people already know on some level that monkey paw and genie lamps are bad, and ai is a lot like this

re 5: the vulnerable world hypothesis is novel and intuitive enough. people can appreciate how devastatingly maximally humanity has tried to inflict damage before, limited only by its weakness. and notice how we are about to get a lot more powerful

text:
ethics is not just about “ranking all the options available to you as an individual by what maximises utility for you in the moment”

that is just one very particular academic perspective of ethics, that became our default in the west because it works so well for technocapital and vibes with materialism

ethics is *fundamentally* about multiplayer cooperation

when you're multiplayer and trying to cooperate, additional concerns arise

- how do you make yourself legible to others so they can model you?
- how do you make the norms predictable? not always locally optimal, but predictable, even to less Intellectually Superior individuals, in ways that degrade gracefully
- how do you make norms robust to perturbation? a slight deviation in conditions, or knowledge of them, or compute allocated to reason about it shouldn't lead to disastrously misaligned actions

the “red” equilibrium fails the above tests with disastrous consequences

text:
things like a trembling hand equilibrium matter. even if you tried to tell everyone to pick red, they might slip up or change heart and pick blue. blue is the equilibrium that saves those whose hands trembled too

it may be achievable to supercooperate with other supercooperators also following this line of reasoning and get to >50% blue

there may be a small chance children, the infirm, and basically anyone without an STEM education pick blue in you haven't pre-briefed them to pick the Game Theoretically Rational Schelling Point

so what do you do? you pick the human equilibrium, which is unfortunately sensitive to the exact framing of the question, which is >50% blue

text:
there is no system of ethics where get to as an individual arbitrarily rewrite the population

in game theory, you don't get to say, “but if only >50% picked blue, we could all live!”

in Game Theory land, picking blue is *defecting*, because you failed to save yourself, and your life is something other people valued

(not technically defecting, but failing to successfully supercooperate anyway)

similarly, in the real world, you are living in a real world a real population of individuals that have already messily tried to figure out some focal points for cooperation

text:
Human values are what they are... a messy set of features but deeply encoded in our psyches

The projection onto these shared weights matters

The framing of the problem deeply shapes how the projection lands onto our shared human values

We don't get to opt out of how things are. We are in continuity with the past. We are contingent on what's happened

Our existing set of values, even if imperfect, are written into billions of humans and our text corpora and the priors of LLMs

These values can be and are subject to change, but they are certainly not ignorable

They should be wished away only at your peril 

They are some of the most important deployed organising principles that exist for cooperation as it exists in our society

text:
B's individual steps may each be plausible, but as a sequence, the final sequence fails to cover the full distribution of plausible sequences

an example: if you have a weighted p=0.75 coin where if you flip heads, you have to flip it again, you can argue flipping the coin once will lead to flipping it again, which will lead to flipping it again, and so if you start, you will never stop flipping coins

text:
misc live notes from a talk by @josh_loughman on "The Structure of Knowledge"

* Reality is messy, our knowledge of it is lossy, we need to organise it some way
* Disciplines as knowledge management systems
* In ancient times, we optimised for oral transmission and stability
* In print era, began having fixity of knowledge, books, cross-references, corpuses and canons
* In 19th century, disciplines began crystallising, professional societies formed
* In 20th century, more math around citation counting, digital citation hunting, seams beginning to crack
* Storage, transmission, dialect, method, trust as relevant factors for knowledge

text:
* Every prior tool externalised a piece of knowing, AI externalises the _knower_ (knowledge that works without being understood, first useful post-human-known knowledge)
* Not too different from specialising; we benefit from society without knowing how to put buildings or computers together, doing our little perspectival jobs

text:
the "absolute maximum compressibility" of a real world data stream is fundamentally ill-defined

it can only ever be defined relative to a particular observer and their knowledge of the universe

there may always be some hidden structure you didn't know about. that would let you have perceive additional structure about the data and compress it even further

maximum compressibility is only defined for data streams of mathematical origin, or more generally, relative to given hypothesis about a data generation process

text:
inoculation prompting works because every piece of text the LLM has ever consumed has some probability mass overall in the data

the model has had to put it somewhere

the most efficient way it found was to put it along some general "misalignment" axis

raw.full_text:
inoculation prompting works because every piece of text the LLM has ever consumed has some probability mass overall in the data

the model has had to put it somewhere

the most efficient way it found was to put it along some general "misalignment" axis

text: when you fine-tune LLMs, you are asking them (mathematically) to find the laziest bit flips to their data generation model to now predict a world in which your fine-tuning text is more likely

raw.full_text: when you fine-tune LLMs, you are asking them (mathematically) to find the laziest bit flips to their data generation model to now predict a world in which your fine-tuning text is more likely

text:
if you fine-tune on inoculation prompts, you then invoke the Superpower

the laziest bit flip when fine-tuning on inoculation prompts is rearrange the probabilities so insecure code is what you get when explicitly asked for

the model is able to assign the probability mass of insecure code behind this gate

without inoculation, the probability mass of generating insecure code gets placed into the "am i being a good person or not" condition (the next most efficient place to put it, thanks to biases in the pretraining distribution and "i am a good assistant" posttraining)

without even posttraining, the model has to spread out the probability mass of insecure code in its overall unconditioned propensities
